title: Mini-batch kernel $k$-means
abstract:
We present the first mini-batch kernel $k$-means algorithm, offering an order of magnitude improvement in running time compared to the full batch algorithm. A single iteration of our algorithm takes $\widetilde{O}(kb^2)$ time, significantly faster than the $O(n^2)$ time required by the full batch kernel $k$-means, where $n$ is the dataset size and $b$ is the batch size. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our algorithm consistently achieves a 10-100x speedup with minimal loss in quality, addressing the slow runtime that has limited kernel $k$-means adoption in practice. We further complement these results with a theoretical analysis under an early stopping condition, proving that with a batch size of $\widetilde{\Omega}(\max \set{\gamma^{4}, \gamma^{2}}\cdot k\epsilon^{-2} )$, the algorithm terminates in $O(\gamma^2/\epsilon)$ iterations with high probability, where $\gamma$ bounds the norm of points in feature space and $\epsilon$ is a termination threshold. Our analysis holds for any reasonable center initialization, and when using $k$-means++ initialization, the algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of $O(\log k)$ in expectation. 
For normalized kernels, such as Gaussian or Laplacian it holds that $\gamma=1$. Taking $\epsilon = O(1)$ and $b=\Theta(k\log n)$, the algorithm terminates in $O(1)$ iterations, with each iteration running in $\widetilde{O}(k^3)$ time.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper presents the first mini-batch kernel k-means algorithm, addressing the computational bottleneck that has limited kernel k-means adoption in practice. The proposed method combines a recursive distance update rule with a truncation technique. The authors provide theoretical guarantees, showing that the algorithm converges in $\mathcal{O}(\gamma^2 / \epsilon)$ iterations with high probability, where $\gamma$ bounds the norm of points in feature space. Experimental results across multiple datasets demonstrate significant speedups over the full-batch kernel k-means while maintaining solution quality, and the method consistently outperforms kernel sketching approaches.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

1.	This is the first mini-batch kernel k-means algorithm which addresses the computational bottleneck limiting kernel k-means adoption.
2.	This method incorporates a recursive distance update rule with a truncation technique to avoid explicit center maintenance and achieve significant speedups while maintaining solution quality.
3.	Authors provide convergence guarantees under early stopping conditions, and showing that the algorithm converges in $\mathcal{O}(\gamma^2 / \epsilon)$ iterations with high probability.

Weaknesses:

1.	You don’t show the experimental results to demonstrate your method can converge quickly. 
2.	Certain statements are not clear. For example, in line 253-255, does the truncated algorithm mean “Truncated beta-MiniBatch Kernel” and the non-truncated version mean the rest ones?
3.	Some typos and grammatical errors.

QUESTIONS:
1.	See weaknesses. 
2.	In line 94, you mentioned “Our algorithm copes better with non linearly separable data”. Can you provide some examples to prove it?
3.	In Figure 3, can you clarify which is your method and the baselines?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes a mini batch kernel k-means algorithm and it improves the running time by an order of magnitude compared to traditional batch algorithms. The experiment shows that the algorithm can consistently achieve a speed improvement of 10-100 times with minimal quality loss.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
1. Provided theoretical analysis including termination conditions, iteration times, and expected approximation ratios, enhancing the credibility and predictability of the algorithm.
2. Through kernel methods, this algorithm can effectively handle non-linear separable data, expanding the application scope of k-means algorithm.
3. The new algorithm has an order of magnitude improvement in running time compared to traditional methods, making the kernel k-means algorithm more practical for processing large-scale datasets.

Weaknesses:
1. Besides the k-means++initialization method, are there any other better initialization strategies that can further improve the performance and convergence speed of the algorithm?
2. How to better integrate this algorithm with other data preprocessing or post-processing techniques (such as feature selection, dimensionality reduction, etc.) to further improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the entire clustering process?

QUESTIONS:
See Weaknesses for details.

LIMITATIONS:
The paper does not discuss its limitations or potential negative impacts. Including a brief discussion on these aspects is recommended.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes the first minibatch-based acceleration method for kernel k-means. k-means is one of the most widely used unsupervised clustering methods, and various acceleration methods have been proposed for it. In recent years, theoretical results on the size of the mini-batch and the number of iterative updates have become known for empirical algorithms utilizing mini-batches that can be applied to large data sets. However, standard k-means only works ideally in limited scenarios, and kernel k-means are promising for more complex real data. This paper successfully elucidates empirically and theoretically that mini-batch speedup works for kernel k-means by using a recursive method to compute the distance between cluster centers and data represented by linear combinations.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
[Strengths]

- This paper follows the recent theoretical analysis of the k-means algorithm using mini-batches [Schwartzman, ICLR2023], and is the first successful speed-up and theoretical analysis of the kernel k-means using mini-batches.

- The paper theoretically demonstrates that kernel k-means not only qualitatively improves clustering with its kernel method (under natural scenarios), but also that mini-batch updates contribute significantly to speedup over standard (non-kernelized) k-means.

[Weaknesse]

- The technical advances in this paper are largely due to existing research, and the kernelization part of the advances appears to be a straightforward application of martingale theory of standard kernel methods.

QUESTIONS:
This paper provides a solid improvement in the evolutionary flow of k-means and kernel k-means in terms of its speedup and theoretical transparency of the number of iterations required. We believe the scientific contribution of this paper, including its active outreach to open source, is of great value. On the other hand, I also have some concerns from two perspectives: the big picture and the local (technical) perspective.The first is that, in the big picture, this paper appears at first glance to be a straightforward kernel extension of the literature  [Schwartzman, ICLR2023]. From a very penetrating point of view, it seems that the part about k-means with mini-batches is a contribution to the literature  [Schwartzman, ICLR2023], and the kernel extension part is a straightforward application of established theory. In other words, the fusion of mini-batch k-means and kernelization does not seem to create any new technical challenges.Another concern is that the presentation of the theoretical analysis itself may have consciously or unconsciously introduced exhibits or assumptions that slightly favor the proposed method. This may be my misunderstanding, so I would appreciate it if you could confirm that my following questions are correct, just in case. Once again, I am grateful to the authors for sharing their excellent paper and for the opportunity to have this discussion.


[Major concern 1: Independence of input data dimension]

The observations authors make in lines 93-97 are conceptually very impressive, but I am somewhat confused. My understanding is that in the proposed method, mini-batch k-means, once and for all, the computation of the inner product of pairs of data requires a computation time that depends on the dimension of the input data. However, I am convinced that there is indeed no dependence on the data dimension k, because for subsequent updates there is no need to perform this inner product calculation. Does the authors' argument implicitly assume that the computation of the prior Gram matrix is not a computational time bottleneck for subsequent iterative updates? Or is my understanding above incorrect, implying that the authors' method can omit even the computation of such a prior Gram matrix? I may have missed some important explanation and would be very grateful for clarification from the authors.


[Major concern 2: Technical novelty]

I would like to better understand the technical novelty/non-obviousness of this paper, so let me ask the following simple question. This paper certainly seems to be the first paper on speedup using mini-batch for kernel k-means (in fact, I have not been able to find a similar technique). However, the algorithms proposed by this paper (in particular Algorithm 1, not Algorithm 2 using truncation) also seem very straightforward. Put another way, it does not appear that Algorithm 1 overcomes any significant obstacles that have traditionally been unresolved over the years. So, at first glance, it would not seem surprising that a similar type of algorithm already existed empirically. Do the authors have any guesses as to why the same type of algorithm has not been utilized until now? For example, is there a reason that the implementation of Algorithm 1 with recursive computation is much more complicated than other algorithms for k-means and kernel k-means if one tries to implement it straightforwardly?



[Minor comments]

> (Lines 185-186) Next, we go a step further and show that it is possible to get an update time with only polylogarithmic dependence on n.

I could not figure out why each iteration requires polylogarithm computation time of data size n in cluster-centered linear combination truncation. At first glance this does not seem to be consistent with the description in lines 226-227, but perhaps my understanding is incorrect?

> (Line 95) ... requires a smaller batch size (Omega(1/epsilon^2) vs Omega((d/epsilon)^2))...

Is the latter complexity Omega((d/epsilon)^2) correct? As stated in the 4-th footnote, authos point out that Omega((d/epsilon)^2) described in [Schwartzman, ICLR2023] contains mistakes and should be replaced with Omega(k(d/epsilon)^2). I would like authors to clarify the correct description, to be sure.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes. Authors clearly discuss its potential limitations.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper presents a new algorithm for mini-batched kernel k-means. The proposed algorithm offer an order of magnitude improvement in running time compared to the full batch algorithm. Theoretical analysis is provided to bound the running time and approximation ratio.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
The results of the paper are very promising. It has been tested on a wide range of datasets including MNIST, PenDigits, Letters, and HAR. The scores are maintained well, and significant speedup is observed.

Section 4 is pretty hard to parse. I wish the paper is written as an algorithm paper. With intuition and algorithms as the main focus. Currently, the paper’s claim is pretty theoretical. I do not have the background to check the correctness of the proof.

Overall, my feeling is that this paper is actually acceptable. I think with some revision, this paper can be a great one, since its result is very strong. My main problem about this paper is that the authors should clarify the main contribution. Currently, it is a combination of algorithm, theory, and implementation. I hope the paper can concentrate on one of them. I feel as an algorithm paper, I don't feel the paper gives reader enough intuition on the proposed. algorithm. As an implementation paper, I feel the evaluation section does not have enough information about the implementation and microbenchmarks (e.g., is any new CUDA kernel being developed, where is the current performance bottleneck).

Other feedback:
I am not sure why the paper needs figure 2. To me, a kernel-based method is better in accuracy than pure distance-based method is very clear.

QUESTIONS:
Can you provide more intuition about your algorithm?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Reject

COMMENT:
This paper introduces a mini-batch kernel k-means algorithm to make the original algorithm more amenable to large scale data. It is not immediate to execute this simple idea of mini-batching to the kernel case, and the paper executes the idea cleanly though the result is unsurprising. Reviewers appreciate that there is some theoretical grounding including approximation guarantees as well as complexity and convergence analysis, and I find the dynamic programming trick to be an elegant way to make sure that the gains via mini-batching are not squandered by the ensuing computations. The main idea from my view is the observation that this implementation trick makes a mini-batch version worthwhile to pursue. 

While it is nice that the method outperforms sketching-based approaches, it is unsurprising that a mini-batch version yields speed ups, and the analysis has limited novelty compared to the analysis of non-kernel versions.  Echoing one reviewer's reservations, there is a gap between the empirical evaluations and the methodological contributions.It would be more interesting to show that the theoretical results agree with empirical trends in a thorough study. Moreover, the examples focus on simple benchmarking but readers do not have a sense of when the algorithm may work well or not, and how choices such as truncation, batch size, etc interplay with more realistic, challenging clustering problems that may reveal more sensitivity to batch versions of algorithms. I encourage the authors to account for these constructive comments in preparing a final revised version of the article.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
